--- title: "Visually communicate your scientific data with R" subtitle: "Designing scientific figures" format: clean-revealjs: # code-fold: false from: markdown+emoji code-overflow: wrap # code-block-bg: "#e60000" # code-block-border-left: "#31BAE9" code-summary: "Show code" highlight-style: dracula author: - name: Eric Largy orcid: 0000-0002-6140-9788 email: eric.largy@u-bordeaux.fr affiliations: - ARNA, INSERM U1212, CNRS UMR 5320, Université de Bordeaux - UFR des Sciences Pharmaceutiques, Université de Bordeaux date: last-modified css: theme/custom.css cache: true --- # The Grammar of Graphics {background-color="#184669"} ## A framework for data visualization {auto-animate="TRUE"} Introduced by Leland Wilkinson ([The Grammar of Graphics](https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/0-387-28695-0 "Access provided by U. Bordeaux"), 2005). Graphics can be built up from a set of components adapted to the `ggplot2` package in R by Hadley Wickham ([ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis](https://ggplot2-book.org/), 2016). ![](resources/overview_graphic-1.png) ## A framework for data visualization {auto-animate="TRUE"} ![](resources/overview_graphic-1.png) ## Aesthetics are layered as geometries
on a coordinate system {auto-animate="TRUE"} ![](resources/gg2.png) ## The [data]{.fg style="--col: #cb962a"} to be visualized has one column per variable
and one row per observation {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns3070 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(dplyr) library(data.table) library(DT) library(ggplot2) DT::datatable(mpg) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## [Data]{.fg style="--col: #cb962a"}: the dataset to be visualized initializes the graph {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot(mpg) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_data-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## [Mapping]{.fg style="--col: #4596d3"} of objects to values {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot( mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_mapping-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Creation of geometric graphs from variables by [layers]{.fg style="--col: #17998e"} {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot( mpg, mapping = aes( x = cty, y = hwy ) ) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_layers-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## [Scales]{.fg style="--col: #a5ca0f"} to represent variables on measured dimensions {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot( mpg, mapping = aes( x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class ) ) + geom_point() + scale_colour_viridis_d() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_scales-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## [Facets]{.fg style="--col: #2934b1"} allows generating many subplots {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot( mpg, mapping = aes( x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class ) ) + geom_point() + scale_colour_viridis_d() + facet_grid(year ~ class) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_facets-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## [Coordinates]{.fg style="--col: #c83c19"} allows re-scaling {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot( mpg, mapping = aes( x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class ) ) + geom_point() + scale_colour_viridis_d() + facet_grid(year ~ class) + coord_cartesian( xlim = c(0, 8), ylim = c(10, 50) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_coordinates-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Make it pretty/corporate/legible with a [theme]{.fg style="--col: #ff69b4"} {.code5 auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns4060 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ggplot( mpg, mapping = aes( x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class ) ) + geom_point() + scale_colour_viridis_d() + facet_grid(year ~ class) + coord_cartesian( xlim = c(0, 8), ylim = c(10, 50), clip = "off" ) + theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom", axis.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"), axis.line = element_line(color = "black", size = 0.75), axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black", size = 0.75), legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10), legend.text = element_text(size = 10), strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10) ) + scale_x_continuous( name = "Engine Displacement (L)", breaks = seq(0, 8, 4), limits = c(0, 8), expand = c(0, 0) ) + scale_y_continuous(name = "Highway Miles per Gallon") + labs(colour = "Vehicle Class") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/ggplot_theme-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # Elements of visual communication {background-color="#184669"} ## Visuals are an increasingly important form of science communication Many scientists not well trained in design *principles* for **effective** messaging Visuals can be improved by taking some simple steps before, during, and after their creation ## Goals of this course Use R to create figures from raw data that: - **convey a clear message** - are produced at high throughput in a reproducible way - can be easily tuned to - be adapted to different media (journals, posters, presentations) ## What we will cover - 10 principles of visual communication - Reminders of R basics - Building figures with `ggplot2` - Going further with "add-on" packages (`patchwork`, `ggtext`, `gganimate`) - Application to concrete examples (could be yours!): 🧪 ## Tools and sources - All figures made in R with ggplot with additional packages: - `tidyverse`, `patchwork`, `data.table`, `ggtext`, `scales`, `gt`, `fakir`, `charlatan`, `ggsci`, `colorblindr`, `gapminder`, `gganimate`, `palmerpenguins`, `png`, `plotly`, `ggalt` - Presentation made with revealJS - Biblio - Patterns (N Y). **2020.** *1*, 100141 - Nature Communications. **2020**. *11*, 5444 # This presentation is available on [R.largy.fr](https://r.largy.fr) {background-color="#184669"} # 10 Principles {background-color="#ffdae0 "} # Principle 1: Diagram first {background-color="#ffdae0 "} ## Focus on information and message Least technical but very important step [**Prioritize the information you want to share**]{.fg style="--col: #ffdae0 "} and design it. - What [**core information**]{.fg style="--col: #ffdae0 "} needs to be conveyed? - What about that information is going to make your point? - Objective: comparison, ranking, trend? - Search the literature for inspiration You can use the grammar of graphics approach to pick the different necessary elements # Principle 2: Use the right software {background-color="#ffdae0 "} ## Excel might not be the best tool - A spreadsheet program may not be sufficient - Data summary by groups - Non-linear fitting, advanced statistics - Examples - Origin - GraphPad Prism - Tableau - MATLAB - Python - [**R**]{.fg style="--col: #ffdae0 "} # Principle 3: Use an Effective Geometry and Show Data {background-color="#ffdae0 "} ## Less is more - Geometries = representations of data in different forms - Often more than one possible geometry - High data-ink ratios[^1] are best: Remove as much non-data-ink as possible (Principle 8) - Unnecessary plot elements (grids, colored background) - Busy axis (too many breaks) - Do not include too much information - Stick to your message (see principle 1) [^1]: ratio of ink used on data compared with overall ink used in a figure ## Geometries - 4 categories - Amounts (or comparisons) - Compositions (or proportions) - Distributions - Relationships - One geometry may work in more than one category - One dataset may be visualized with more than one geometry ## Amounts - Examples: - Bar plots - Scatter plots - Heatmaps - Only for data without - Distributional information - Uncertainty ## Bar plots - Bar plots are popular but often a bad choice - Low data density - Always start at zero, they can be misleading - Part of the range covered by the bar might have never been observed - Good use of a bar plot: show counts of something - Poor use of a bar plot: show group means ## Bar plots: showing counts Example with a [Drug review dataset from WebMD](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/rohanharode07/webmd-drug-reviews-dataset): - How many counts of each *Ease of use* score? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Age Condition Date Drug DrugId EaseofUse Effectiveness Satisfaction UsefulCount Gender
55-64 Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus 5/3/2014 metformin hcl 11285 2 3 1 48 Female
45-54 Bipolar Depression 12/29/2012 geodon 20575 2 2 1 6 Female
55-64 Enlarged Prostate 11/7/2013 terazosin hcl 6834 4 4 3 24 Male
25-34 Genital Herpes 1/10/2009 valtrex 14126 4 2 1 2 Female
45-54 Muscle Pain and Stiffness in Shoulder, Neck and Pelvis 1/2/2014 prednisone concentrate 6007 5 5 5 2 NA
35-44 Bacterial Meningitis caused by Streptococcus 11/14/2008 penicillin g potassium vial 5850 1 1 3 0 Male
``` ::: ::: ## Bar plots: showing counts Bar plot shows data counts with very low information density ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/bar plot ex-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: What differences do you notice? Which is best? Would you change anything? ## Compositions Goal: For each *Ease of use* score, what is the proportion of females/males? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/pies-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: - Pie charts are a classic that **do not work** in most cases - Inherent difficulties in making visual comparisons - Prefer e.g., stacked or clustered bar plots ## Stacked and clustered bars For each *Ease of use* score, what is the proportion of females/males? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/clusterd and stacked-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: Is the message clear? What would you change? ## Normalized bars - *Normalized* bars better show the composition - Loss of information on counts - But was it the intended message? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/normalized bars-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Different message = change of aesthetics For each gender, what is the proportion of *Ease of use* ? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/propo-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Distributions - Underused category - High data density - Examples - Box plot - Histogram - Violin plot - Density plot - Better to show the data ## Distributions - Example of a [water temperature in Antibes dataset](https://www.data.gouv.fr/fr/datasets/temperature-de-leau-de-mer-a-antibes-juan-les-pins/) - Goal: compare temperatures across years - 6 first data points: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Date Temperature (°C)
2023-07-21 28.0
2020-09-16 23.8
2021-06-15 23.8
2022-05-24 22.0
2021-07-07 25.0
2021-06-16 24.1
``` ::: ::: ## Bar plots: showing mean :vomiting_face: No distribution information (only mean) and suggests data starts at 0! ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/geom_col-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Introducing data spread info Using error bars, but what do they represent here? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/errorbars-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Scatter is less misleading But the distribution information is still lacking ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/misled-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Is mean ± sd not enough? Still insufficient to differentiate these (fake) distributions ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/rnormy-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Box plots are much better Box plots show the *spread* of data through their quartiles. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/boxyboxes-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Data points may be added *Density* can be shown with individual data points with *transparency* ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/box and points-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Data points may be added Or with *jitter* ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/jittery-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Data points may be added Or *both* ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/transparentjitter-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Violin plots Violin plots even more effective to compare (probability) distributions ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/jitteredviolins-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Violin + boxes Quantiles can be shown directly of via superimposed boxes ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/violin and box-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Relationship - Most presentations of x- and y-coordinate data - Scatter plots are simple and effective - Lines may be shown - Information can be layered - symbols (e.g. circles, squares) - size - color - line type (e.g. dashed vs. plain) ## Scatter plot Temperature vs. time with *points* ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/scatterplot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Scatter plot Temperature vs. time with *points* and *line* ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/plot and lines-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Scatter plot Temperature vs. time with *points* and *smoothed line* ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/smoothed-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Penguin dataset ::::: columns ::: {.column width="60%"} - Source: [Palmer station penguins dimensions](https://github.com/allisonhorst/palmerpenguins) - Repository and Artwork by @allison_horst ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g sex year
Gentoo Biscoe 50.5 15.9 222 5550 male 2008
Adelie Biscoe 35.7 16.9 185 3150 female 2008
Chinstrap Dream 51.3 18.2 197 3750 male 2007
Adelie Dream 40.8 18.9 208 4300 male 2008
Adelie Torgersen 38.9 17.8 181 3625 female 2007
Gentoo Biscoe 50.5 15.2 216 5000 female 2009
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](resources/penguins.png){width="75%"} ![](resources/culmen_depth.png){width="75%"} ::: ::::: ## Distributions Bad and good ways to plot distributions ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/distripenguins-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Bubble plot Adds a symbol scale aesthetics to map an additional variable ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/bubble plot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Lollipop plot (Lolliplot) Analogous to a barplot but less misleading ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/lollipop-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Dumbbell plot Multiple comparisons between two points. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/dumbbell-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: # Principle 4: Colors mean something {background-color="#ffdae0 "} ## Color is often the first visual information
getting across to the reader - Colors represent information - in a direct and obvious way, - or in an indirect and subtle way - Must make the message easier to understand - Not just to make the figure pretty **Colors should be strategically used to amplify your visual message** ## Classes disinguish between
sequential and diverging schemes ![[Source](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-19160-7)](resources/color_classes.png){fig.cap="right aligned" fig-align="center"} ## Types distinguish between
continuous, discrete, and categorical data ![[Source](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-19160-7)](resources/color_types.png){fig.cap="right aligned" fig-align="center"} ## Sequential coloring can be light to dark
with one hue ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/single hue-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Sequential coloring can be light to dark
with several hues ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/multiple hues-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Diverging coloring uses two sequential schemes
and one neutral color ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/div colors-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Using meaningful coloring may improve understanding ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/obv-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Categorical coloring involves unrelated colors
to convey qualitative group differences {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: data-id="id_01" ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/qual-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ## Meaningful colors may not be culturally obvious
or appropriate for everyone {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: data-id="id_01" ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/qual2-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ## Highlighting discrete values can be very effective
to convey a message ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/high-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Highlighting discrete values can be very effective
to convey a message ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/high2-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Data highlighting can be reinfored by *few* other elements ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/high3-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Design figures that work for everyone - Use colors that can be distinguished by colorblind people - [Okabe Ito](https://mikemol.github.io/technique/colorblind/2018/02/11/color-safe-palette.html) - Use color schemes that are perceptually uniform to prevent data distortion - [Viridis](https://waldyrious.net/viridis-palette-generator/), [Cividis](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0199239), [Brewer](https://colorbrewer2.org/#type=sequential&scheme=BuGn&n=3) - Combine colors with symbols, line type and other elements to convey same information - Use effective redundancy to increase understanding and accessibility - Use transparency instead of color for density, background info ## Use effective redundancy to increase understanding and accessibility Note: use of symbols, direct labeling in title and inset figure (same color, same order) ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/penguins-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Default colors may cause issues for colorblind people ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/bad choices-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Meaningful colors may also be problematic ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/bad choices 2-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## [Okabe Ito](https://mikemol.github.io/technique/colorblind/2018/02/11/color-safe-palette.html) is designed for colorblindness ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/okabe gray-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## [Viridis](https://waldyrious.net/viridis-palette-generator/) is always a good choice ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/cividis gray-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## [Cividis](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0199239) as well ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/viridis gray-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Data variation is under- or overrepresented with rainbows ![[Source](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-19160-7)](resources/jet.png) ## So how do I pick a color palette? ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} Class and type ![](resources/palette%20selection%201.png){width="100%"} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} Colour combination ![](resources/palette%20selection%202.png){width="100%"} ::: ::::: [Source](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-19160-7) # Principle 5: Include Uncertainty {background-color="#ffdae0 "} ## Uncertainty is an inherent part of understanding most systems Failure to include uncertainty can be **misleading** Two primary issues with uncertainty: - Failure to include uncertainty = doubts over other parts of the statistical message - e.g. inference on the mean - Misrepresentation (or misinterpretation) of uncertainty ## There are simple ways to include uncertainty in your figures - Classicly: - Error bars - Shaded intervals (ribbons) - Or implicitly, with: - Box plots - Violins - Distributions ## Difficult to assess significance
when uncertainty is omitted ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/omitted uncertainty-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Error bars are simple but hide data distribution ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/error bars-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Transparency shows data density effectively ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/all data points-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Violin/boxes highlight distribution characteristics ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/all data points with violin-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Color can help but should be meaningful ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/all data points with violin colored-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Fitting without uncertainty... ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/fit no uncertainty-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Doesn't really tell the full story ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/not full story-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Uncertainty with ribbon for lines ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/uncertain ribbon-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Integrating uncertainty with other elements can be effective ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/more ribbons-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Many common metrics but each represents a different measure - Standard deviation - Spread of data: information about entire population - Includes range of expected new values - Standard error - Uncertainty in mean (or other estimate) - Strongly influenced by sample size - Confidence intervals - To display reliability of measurement Responsibility of author to adopt the most appropriate measure When possible: show the data! ## Be cautious with interpretation A gap between (error) bars does not ensure significance, nor does overlap rule it out --- it depends on the type of bar Be very clear in stating: - what type of uncertainty you are sharing - what the interpretation is # Principle 6: Panels {background-color="#ffdae0 "} ## Faceting is a powerful way
to split data into digestible chunks Advice: - Do not repeat unnecessary axis - Keep axis scales identical as far as possible - Different scales can be misleading - If it possible to facet with two variables: make a grid! - If the information is complete enough ## Starting from a classical scatterplot ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/penguinsbase-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Free scales accomodate data variation
but can be misleading ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/facet penguins free-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Fixed scales allow for direct comparisons ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/facet penguins-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Are symbol colors and shapes still necessary? ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/facet penguins_2-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Colors can be used to map an additional variable ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/facet penguins more vars-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Exercice: Conveying a message by faceting Example data [Surface water quality in Loire-Atlantique](https://www.data.gouv.fr/fr/datasets/suivi-qualite-des-eaux-superficielles-en-loire-atlantique-stations-du-reseau-departemental/) ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
date code_station city city_group parameter value
2009-06-03 04215000 Issé CC Châteaubriant-Derval Température de l'Eau 18.42
2010-07-28 04136400 Oudon CC du Pays d'Ancenis Conductivité à 25°C 508.00
2013-02-26 04146900 Nantes Nantes Métropole Nitrates 14.10
2016-10-18 04136400 Oudon CC du Pays d'Ancenis Ammonium 0.26
2008-11-26 04215930 Fégréac CA Redon Agglomération Azote Kjeldahl 1.60

``` ::: ::: Goal: Highlight *where* (in which station) and *when* the pH deviated from 7 ## Facetting might be necessary ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/no faceting-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Facets should be simplified ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/faceting-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: What improvements could be made? ## Highlighting, axis simplification, scaling, grid elements ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-revealjs/faceting + colors + transparency-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: Color *highlights*, axis simplification, scaling, grid elements ## Faceting statistics summary Goal: *Where* (groups of cities) did the pH significantly deviate (e.g., more than 1 SD) *over a year* compared to other years? The `data.table` alternative for large dataset and concise syntax {auto-animate="TRUE"} Think in terms of basic units: [**rows**]{.fg style="--col: #184669"}, [**columns**]{.fg style="--col: #0c9b1f"}, and [**groups**]{.fg style="--col: #cc7504"}. **DT\[[i]{.fg style="--col: #184669"}, [j]{.fg style="--col: #0c9b1f"}, [by]{.fg style="--col: #cc7504"}\]** - [**i**]{.fg style="--col: #184669"}: row selection (filtering) - [**j**]{.fg style="--col: #0c9b1f"}: column selection (projection) - [**by**]{.fg style="--col: #cc7504"}: grouping operations `data.table` is optimized for speed and memory, ideal for large datasets ## The `data.table` alternative for large dataset and concise syntax {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns6040 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} a_dt <- data.table( id = 1:5, group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "C"), value = c(10.2, 12.5, 9.8, 11.1, 13.0) ) a_dt ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` id group value 1: 1 A 10.2 2: 2 A 12.5 3: 3 B 9.8 4: 4 B 11.1 5: 5 C 13.0 ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns6040 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # FROM[WHERE, SELECT, GROUP BY] # DT [i, j, by] # Select rows where group is "A" a_dt[group == "A"] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` id group value 1: 1 A 10.2 2: 2 A 12.5 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns6040 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # Select columns by name a_dt[, .(id, value)] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` id value 1: 1 10.2 2: 2 12.5 3: 3 9.8 4: 4 11.1 5: 5 13.0 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns6040 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # Select rows where group is "A" or "B", and value > 10 # Yield the corresponding id values a_dt[ group %in% c("A", "B") & value > 10, .(id) ] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` id 1: 1 2: 2 3: 4 ``` ::: ::: ::: # 10 common pitfalls {background-color="#184669"} And how to avoid them ## R is 1-indexed {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # R is 1-indexed # The first element is at position 1 R_vector <- c("a", "b", "c") R_vector[1] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "a" ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.python .cell-code} # Python is 0-indexed # The first element is at position 0 python_list = ["a", "b", "c"] python_list[0] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 'a' ``` ::: ::: ::: ## \[ \] returns a listis not \[\[ \]\] ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # list of uneven numbers <= 100 b_list <- as.list( which(1:100 %% 2 == 1, 1:100) ) ``` ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # Returns a list b_list[1:3] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 1 [[2]] [1] 3 [[3]] [1] 5 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # Returns an element b_list[[3]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 5 ``` ::: ::: ::: ## Check for missing values with `is.na()` {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} vec_1 <- c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5) vec_1 == NA ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] NA NA NA NA NA ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # NA == NA returns NA # because NA is a missing value, # not a comparable one NA == NA ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] NA ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} is.na(vec_1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE FALSE ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} any(is.na(vec_1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] TRUE ``` ::: ::: ::: ## R coerces all elements to the most flexible type {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} mixed_vector <- c(1, "b", TRUE) mixed_vector ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "1" "b" "TRUE" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} mixed_vector <- c(1, "b", TRUE) class(mixed_vector) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ::: ## R recycles shorter vectors to match the length of longer vectors {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} 1:10 + c(10, 20) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 11 22 13 24 15 26 17 28 19 30 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell .columns5050 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # A warning is issued # only if the lengths are not multiples # of each other 1:5 + c(10, 20) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning in 1:5 + c(10, 20): longer object length is not a multiple of shorter object length ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 11 22 13 24 15 ``` ::: ::: ## `NULL` is different from `NA` {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns7030 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} list_NA_NULL <- list( "missing" = NA, "null_value" = NULL ) list_NA_NULL["length_missing"] <- length(list_NA_NULL[[1]]) list_NA_NULL["length_null"] <- length(list_NA_NULL[[2]]) list_NA_NULL[5] <- length(list_NA_NULL[2]) list_NA_NULL["na_check"] <- is.na(list_NA_NULL["missing"]) list_NA_NULL["null_check"] <- is.null(list_NA_NULL[["null_value"]]) list_NA_NULL["null_check_2"] <- is.null(list_NA_NULL["null_value"]) # length_null = 0 because NULL has no length # [5] = 1 because list_NA_NULL[2] is a list of length 1, not NULL itself # null_check = TRUE because the element inside is NULL # null_check_2 = FALSE because it is a list of length 1 != NULL str(list_NA_NULL) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` List of 8 $ missing : logi NA $ null_value : NULL $ length_missing: int 1 $ length_null : int 0 $ : int 1 $ na_check : logi TRUE $ null_check : logi TRUE $ null_check_2 : logi FALSE ``` ::: ::: ## Adding a factor level does not add data {auto-animate="TRUE"} ::: {.cell .columns7030 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} factor_vec <- factor(c("A", "B", "C")) levels(factor_vec) <- c("A", "B", "C", "D") factor_vec ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] A B C Levels: A B C D ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell .columns7030 output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # Data to add data_to_add <- c("D", "E") # Add new levels to factor_vec and generate new levels for the new data factor_vec <- c( factor_vec, factor( data_to_add, levels = unique(c(levels(factor_vec), data_to_add)) ) ) factor_vec ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] A B C D E Levels: A B C D E ``` ::: ::: ::: ## <script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
